key_takeaways: Power segment delivered 34% order growth with 9 HA units booked in Q3 alone (exceeding all of 2023), and capacity is being expanded to 70-80 heavy-duty gas turbines/year by 2026; 2025 orders expected to match or exceed 2024 levels with a stronger North America/hyperscaler mix.

Electrification achieved double-digit EBITDA margins for the first time with ~700 bps expansion, backlog growing to ~$19B (+$6B YoY), and capacity being doubled largely through lean/asset utilization improvements rather than greenfield CapEx.

Offshore wind remains a significant headwind with a ~$700M charge in Q3 due to blade manufacturing deviations and project delays; the ~$3B remaining backlog burn-down timeline has shifted right, and GEV does not plan to add new offshore backlog without substantially different economics.
risk_flags: Offshore wind blade manufacturing defect (Canada factory) affected a low-single-digit proportion of all manufactured blades; remediation costs and project delays (Vineyard Wind, Dogger Bank) drove ~$700M in additional contract losses in Q3.
Offshore wind backlog completion timeline has moved beyond the previously guided end-2025 target, with updated burn-down schedule to be provided at December 10 Analyst Day.
Onshore wind order inflection in North America remains uncertain; interconnection queue congestion is delaying a return to mid-teens gigawatt annual commissioning levels.
Fatality reported at an onshore wind site in Sweden, highlighting ongoing safety execution risks.
Q4 free cash flow expected to be lower year-over-year due to improved linearity, lower SunZia-type down payments, higher taxes from EBITDA growth, and elevated CapEx for capacity expansion.
